bibliographic_data.title: LncRNA as a Therapeutic Target for Angiogenesis
bibliographic_data.abstract: Background: Out of 3 billion base pairs in human genome only ~2% code for proteins; and out of 180,000 transcripts in human cells, about 20,000 code for protein, remaining 160,000 are non-coding transcripts. Most of these transcripts are more than 200 base pairs and constitute a group of long non-coding RNA (lncRNA). Many of the lncRNA have its own promoter, and are well con- served in mammals. Accumulating evidence indicates that lncRNAs act as molecular switches in cellular differentiation, movement, apoptosis, and in the reprogramming of cell states by altering gene expression patterns. However, the role of this important group of molecules in angiogenesis is not well understood. Angiogenesis is a complex process and depends on precise regulation of gene expression. Conclusion: Dysregulation of transcription during this process may lead to several diseases including various cancers. As angiogenesis is an important process in cancer pathogenesis and treatment, lncRNA may be playing an important role in angiogenesis. In support of this, lncRNA microvascular invasion in hepatocellular carcinoma (MVIH) has been shown to activate angiogenesis. Furthermore, lncRNA-Meg3-knockout mouse showed increased expression of vascular endothelial growth factor pathway genes and increased cortical microvessel density. Overall, there is strong evidence that lncRNA is an important class of regulatory molecule, and a number of studies have demonstrated that these can be targeted to change cellular physiology and functions. In this review, we have attempted to summarize these studies and elucidate the potential of this novel regulatory molecule as a therapeutic target.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper discusses the role of long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) in angiogenesis, highlighting their potential as therapeutic targets for diseases related to dysregulated transcription during this process.
core_content.key_methodology: The review summarizes existing studies on lncRNAs and their functions, particularly focusing on their roles in cellular processes such as gene expression regulation and angiogenesis.
outcomes: lncRNA microvascular invasion in hepatocellular carcinoma (MVIH) activates angiogenesis
lncRNA-Meg3-knockout mouse shows increased vascular endothelial growth factor pathway genes and cortical microvessel density
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper explores the role of long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) in cellular processes, particularly focusing on their potential as therapeutic targets for diseases related to dysregulated transcription during angiogenesis. The authors discuss how lncRNAs act by regulating gene expression at both the transcriptional and post-transcriptional levels. They highlight several studies that demonstrate the importance of lncRNAs in various biological activities such as reprogramming cell states, cellular differentiation, movement, apoptosis, and angiogenesis. Specifically, they mention the role of lncRNA microvascular invasion in hepatocellular carcinoma (MVIH) in activating angiogenesis and the increased expression of vascular endothelial growth factor pathway genes and cortical microvessel density observed in a lncRNA-Meg3-knockout mouse model.
